text: Personal data must be collected for specified, explicit and legitimate purposes and not further processed in a manner that is incompatible with those purposes.

Personal data must be kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed. Data controllers must define retention periods.
Where processing is based on consent, the controller must be able to demonstrate that the data subject has consented. The request for consent must be presented in a clear and plain language.

At the time when personal data are obtained, the controller shall provide the data subject with information including the purposes and legal basis for processing, recipients, retention period, and rights of the data subject.
The data subject shall have the right to obtain erasure of personal data without undue delay. The controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay.
A consumer shall have the right to request that a business disclose the categories and specific pieces of personal information it has collected about that consumer.
A consumer shall have the right to direct a business that sells or shares personal information to third parties to not sell or share the consumer's personal information (opt-out right).
Unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce are unlawful. This includes misleading terms of service, hidden fees, and automatic renewal without adequate notice.
Consumers have the right to withdraw from a contract within 14 days without giving any reason. Traders must inform consumers about this right in plain and clear language.

A term of a consumer contract is unfair if, contrary to the requirement of good faith, it causes a significant imbalance in the parties' rights and obligations under the contract, to the detriment of the consumer.

Limitation of liability clauses should be reasonable and proportionate. Courts may strike down clauses that exclude liability for gross negligence or attempt to limit liability in a disproportionate way.
Termination clauses should specify the notice period required, grounds for termination, and consequences of termination including data handling and fee obligations.
Every contract should specify the governing law and jurisdiction to prevent disputes about which legal system applies. This is especially important for cross-border agreements.
License grants to user-generated content should be limited to what is necessary for service operation. Broad assignments of IP rights without compensation may be challenged as unconscionable.
The controller and processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including encryption and pseudonymisation.
Operators of websites and online services directed to children under 13 must obtain verifiable parental consent before collecting personal information from children.
Companies must clearly disclose auto-renewal terms before purchase, provide easy cancellation mechanisms, and send reminders before renewal charges are processed.
Personal data must be processed only for lawful purposes and with valid consent from the data principal. Consent must be free, specific, informed, and unambiguous.
Data fiduciaries must process personal data only for the purpose specified at the time of collection and cannot use it for unrelated purposes without fresh consent.
Personal data should not be retained beyond the purpose for which it was collected. Data must be deleted once the purpose is fulfilled unless retention is required by law.
Organizations must implement reasonable security safeguards to prevent data breaches and must notify authorities and affected individuals in case of a breach.
Companies handling sensitive personal data must implement reasonable security practices. Failure leading to wrongful loss or gain can result in liability.
Unfair contract terms that cause significant imbalance to the consumer may be declared void. Businesses must ensure transparency and fairness in terms.
Refund policies must clearly specify timelines, eligibility conditions, and procedures. Ambiguous refund clauses may be considered unfair trade practice.
E-commerce entities must clearly display terms of sale, refund, return, warranty, and grievance redressal mechanisms.
E-commerce platforms must appoint a grievance officer and provide contact details for consumer complaints.
